locked_facts: Coulomb's law: F = kq₁q₂/r², where k = 9 × 10⁹ N·m²/C²
Force is along the line joining the two charges (central force)
Like charges repel, unlike charges attract
Force obeys inverse square law: doubling distance reduces force to one-quarter
Superposition principle: net force = vector sum of individual Coulomb forces
In a medium with dielectric constant K, force reduces by factor K: F_medium = F_vacuum/K
truth_statements: The electrostatic force between two point charges is directly proportional to the product of magnitudes of the charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them
Coulomb's law holds exactly for point charges and is analogous to Newton's law of gravitation in form
The principle of superposition states that the force on any charge due to multiple charges is the vector sum of forces due to each charge individually
The force between two charges in a medium is reduced by the dielectric constant of the medium
The electrostatic force is a conservative force — work done is path-independent

aha_moment.what_to_show: Two positive charges with force vectors drawn exactly along the line joining them — as student drags one charge closer, the force arrows grow by the inverse-square factor, quadrupling when distance halves
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Force arrows scaling as 1/r² with numerical readout updating in real time
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see the force is always along r̂ (not along the field direction at that point) and changes dramatically with distance

simulation_states.label: Two charges at rest
simulation_states.description: Two point charges (+q and +q) placed at fixed positions with no force vectors shown. Charge labels visible. The field lines are faintly drawn radiating outward from each charge.
simulation_states.emphasis: Establish the setup — two positive charges separated by distance r

simulation_states.label: Force vectors appear
simulation_states.description: Force arrows appear on each charge pointing away from the other (repulsion). The arrows are exactly along the line joining the charges. A formula overlay shows F = kq₁q₂/r².
simulation_states.emphasis: Force direction is along the line joining charges — not along field lines at the surface

simulation_states.label: Inverse-square demonstration
simulation_states.description: Distance r halves — force arrows quadruple in length. Numerical readout shows F going from F₀ to 4F₀. The force vectors remain exactly along the charge-joining line even as field lines curve.
simulation_states.emphasis: Force quadruples when distance halves — inverse square law made viscerally visible

simulation_states.label: Interactive exploration
simulation_states.description: Student drags charges and adjusts q₁, q₂ values. Force arrows and numerical readout update in real time. Switching one charge to negative shows attraction (arrows reverse). A third charge can be added to demonstrate superposition.
simulation_states.emphasis: Try like vs unlike charges, vary distance, add a third charge to see superposition

common_misconceptions.belief: The electric force acts in the direction of the electric field at that point, not along the line joining the charges
common_misconceptions.correction: Coulomb's force between two point charges always acts along the straight line joining them — this is a central force, independent of the field pattern around them
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show force arrows rigidly along r̂ while field lines curve around the charges

common_misconceptions.belief: Doubling the distance halves the force
common_misconceptions.correction: Force follows an inverse-square law: doubling distance reduces force to one-quarter, not one-half
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Animate distance doubling and show force arrow shrinking to 1/4 its original size with numerical readout
